501: Founder of the Akkadian Empire, considered the first multiethnic empire in history.
502: Pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty, one of the few women to rule Egypt as a full-fledged king.
503: Conquering pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty, sometimes called 'the Napoleon of Egypt'.
504: Pharaoh who imposed the monotheistic cult of Aten; husband of Nefertiti and father of Tutankhamun.
505: King of Babylon, conqueror of Jerusalem and builder of the Hanging Gardens.
506: Founder of the Achaemenid Empire, known for his religious and administrative tolerance.
507: Achaemenid king under whom the Persian Empire reached its greatest extent; he invaded Greece at Marathon.
508: Persian king who invaded Greece and was defeated at Salamis and Plataea.
509: Last Achaemenid king, defeated by Alexander the Great at Issus and Gaugamela.
510: Archaic Greek poet, author of the 'Theogony' and 'Works and Days'.
511: King of Sparta, celebrated for his defense of the pass of Thermopylae against the Persians.
512: Athenian statesman, architect of the Greek victory at the Battle of Salamis.
513: Athenian general who defeated the Persians at the Battle of Marathon.
514: Athenian politician and general, a controversial figure of the Peloponnesian War.
515: Greek historian and soldier, author of the 'Anabasis' and disciple of Socrates.
516: Pre-Socratic philosopher, the first to bring philosophy to Athens; he proposed the 'Nous' as an ordering principle.
517: Pre-Socratic philosopher who proposed the four elements (earth, water, air, fire).
518: Pre-Socratic philosopher of Elea, defender of the immutability of Being.
519: Pre-Socratic philosopher known for his paradoxes about motion.
520: Greek sophist, author of the famous dictum 'Man is the measure of all things'.
521: Greek sophist and orator, master of rhetoric.
522: Greek sculptor, creator of the Zeus of Olympia and supervisor of the sculptures of the Parthenon.
523: Greek sculptor of the late classical period, creator of the Hermes of Olympia.
524: Greek sculptor, creator of the 'Doryphoros' and theorist of the classical canon of proportions.
525: Greek lyric poet, celebrated for his odes dedicated to the victors of the Panhellenic Games.
526: King of Macedon who reformed his army and unified Greece; father of Alexander the Great.
527: General of Alexander the Great and founder of the Ptolemaic dynasty of Egypt.
528: General of Alexander the Great and founder of the Seleucid Empire after the division of his empire.
529: General of Alexander the Great who attempted to keep his empire united after his death.
530: King of Epirus, celebrated for his 'Pyrrhic victories' against Rome.
531: Founder of Stoic philosophy in Athens.
532: Greek astronomer who first proposed a heliocentric model of the universe.
533: Greek polymath who calculated the circumference of the Earth with remarkable precision.
534: Greek astronomer, discoverer of the precession of the equinoxes and father of trigonometry.
535: Greek mathematician, author of the classical treatise on conic sections.
536: Greco-Egyptian astronomer and geographer, author of the 'Almagest' and the geocentric model.
537: Greco-Roman physician whose work dominated Western medicine for over a thousand years.
538: Neoplatonist philosopher, mathematician, and astronomer; murdered by a Christian mob in Alexandria.
539: Greek historian and biographer, author of the 'Parallel Lives'.
540: Greek geographer and historian, author of the 'Geography' in 17 books.
541: Roman Epicurean poet and philosopher, author of the poem 'De rerum natura'.
542: Roman lyric poet of the 'neoteric' circle.
543: Roman poet of the Augustan era, author of the 'Odes' and 'Ars Poetica'.
544: Roman historian, author of 'Ab Urbe Condita', a monumental history of Rome.
545: Roman historian, author of 'The Lives of the Twelve Caesars'.
546: Roman satirical poet, author of the 'Satires' depicting Roman decadence.
547: Hispano-Roman poet, master of the Latin epigram.
548: Latin writer, author of 'The Golden Ass', the earliest fully surviving Latin novel.
549: Roman consul and dictator, symbol of civic virtue for relinquishing power after saving Rome.
550: Roman consul who devised the strategy of attrition ('cunctator') against Hannibal.
551: Tribune of the plebs who championed agrarian reform; assassinated by the Roman aristocracy.
552: Roman tribune of the plebs, brother of Tiberius; continued reforms until his violent death.
553: Stoic Roman senator, symbol of republican resistance against Julius Caesar.
554: Roman senator, co-organizer of the assassination of Julius Caesar alongside Brutus.
555: General and architect of Augustus, victor at Actium; promoter of the Pantheon in Rome.
556: Advisor to Augustus and patron of poets such as Virgil and Horace; his name became synonymous with patronage of the arts.
557: Roman empress, wife of Augustus and mother of Tiberius; a figure of great political influence.
558: Roman empress and mother of Nero; a key figure of the Julio-Claudian dynasty.
559: Roman empress, third wife of Claudius; famous for her controversial life.
560: First emperor of the Antonine dynasty, who opened the period of the 'Five Good Emperors'.
561: Roman emperor and founder of the Severan dynasty, who militarized the Empire.
562: Roman emperor who reunified the Empire during the crisis of the third century.
563: Last pagan Roman emperor, who attempted to restore Greco-Roman polytheism.
564: Western Roman Emperor under whom the Visigoths sacked Rome in 410 AD.
565: Roman general of Vandal origin, defender of the Empire against the Goths.
566: Visigoth king who conquered and sacked Rome in 410 AD.
567: Barbarian chieftain who in 476 AD deposed the last Western Roman Emperor, Romulus Augustulus.
568: Ostrogothic king of Italy, who ruled after defeating Odoacer and maintained Roman traditions.
569: Roman philosopher, author of 'The Consolation of Philosophy', a bridge between Antiquity and the Middle Ages.
570: Bishop and Church Father, author of 'Confessions' and 'The City of God'.
571: Church Father, translator of the Bible into Latin (the Vulgate).
572: Bishop of Milan and Church Father, mentor of Augustine of Hippo.
573: Christian theologian and philosopher, one of the great thinkers of the Greek patristic tradition.
574: Carthaginian theologian, the first major Christian author to write in Latin.
575: Hispano-Roman bishop and encyclopedist, author of the 'Etymologiae'.
576: Byzantine empress, wife of Justinian I and a figure of enormous political influence.
577: Byzantine emperor who defeated the Sasanian Empire but lost the East to Islam.
578: Byzantine emperor, known as 'Bulgaroktonos' (Bulgar-Slayer) for his campaigns against the Bulgarians.
579: Byzantine princess and historian, author of the 'Alexiad'.
580: Last Byzantine emperor, who died defending Constantinople from the Ottoman army.
581: Ottoman sultan who conquered Constantinople in 1453 AD.
582: Ottoman sultan under whose reign the Empire reached its greatest splendor.
583: First caliph of Islam after the death of Muhammad; he initiated the great Arab conquests.
584: Second caliph, who expanded Islamic dominion over Persia, Egypt, and the Levant.
585: Cousin and son-in-law of Muhammad, fourth caliph and central figure of Shia Islam.
586: Abbasid caliph under whose reign Baghdad was the cultural and scientific center of the Islamic world.
587: Persian mathematician, father of algebra; his name gave rise to the word 'algorithm'.
588: Arab philosopher, the first great thinker of Islam and pioneer of rational thought.
589: Persian physician and polymath, author of pioneering treatises on smallpox and measles.
590: Muslim philosopher called 'the Second Teacher' (after Aristotle).
591: Arab polymath, pioneer of modern optics with his 'Book of Optics'.
592: Persian polymath, astronomer, mathematician, and ethnographer of medieval India.
593: Persian poet, astronomer, and mathematician, author of the 'Rubaiyat'.
594: Sephardic Jewish philosopher and rabbi, author of the 'Guide for the Perplexed'.
595: Persian mystic poet, the foremost exponent of Sufism and author of the 'Masnavi'.
596: Arab historian and philosopher, considered the founder of sociology and modern historiography.
597: First king of the Franks, who unified the Frankish tribes and converted to Christianity.
598: Frankish mayor of the palace who halted the Muslim expansion into Europe at the Battle of Poitiers (732 AD).
599: First Carolingian king of the Franks and father of Charlemagne.
600: King of Wessex who defended England from the Vikings and promoted Anglo-Saxon culture.
